text: The surgeon approaches the hernia site, creating the volcano sign by dissecting and removing preperitoneal fat and the hernia sac.
The surgeon encounters thick, scarred tissue while addressing the recurrent hernia, indicative of previous surgical intervention.
The surgeon excises the burnt skin and reconstructs the patient's umbilicus, completing the procedure with visible precision.
The surgeon navigates to the contralateral side, reaching the end of the preperitoneal fat, and carefully attempts to enter the correct plane to dissect the peritoneum without causing perforations.
The surgeon selects a mesh and positions it intra-abdominally using sutures, then closes the defect and places the mesh into the pre-peritoneal pocket.
The surgeon closes the defect in two layers using a running non-absorbable number one PDS suture, visibly struggling with the eighteen-inch suture during the process.
The surgeon uses scissors to further dissect the inferior aspect of the pre-peritoneal pocket, ensuring that the 15 by 15 centimeter flat polypropylene uncoated mesh can lay flat within the space.

The surgeon places eight tacking sutures, using two Vicryl sutures and three on each side, with two more centrally to secure the mesh against the fascia. A 3-0 barbed suture is used to close the pocket, with Stratofix being utilized due to the unavailability of the preferred V-lock suture.

The surgeon utilizes two eight millimeter ports and one twelve millimeter port for the procedure, closing the fascial defect at the twelve millimeter site. The twelve millimeter port is used to ensure the ability to perform preperitoneal repair and potentially insert mesh through the eight millimeter trocar.
